selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

messages.content: You must return JSON only. No markdown, no code fences, no commentary. Return a single JSON object with keys: mode, topic, opinion, reasoning_summary.

Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):
{"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.","persona":{"age":38,"biological_sex":"female","gender":"woman","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"electrician","city_of_living":"Los Angeles","employment_status":"employed part-time","education_level":"upper secondary","parents_education":{"parent_1":"lower secondary","parent_2":"vocational diploma"},"marital_status":"married","children":3,"migration_status":"native American","psychological_feature":"depressed","religious_beliefs":"Atheist","time_onsocialmedia":"You have no social media","hobbies":["cycling","classical music","volunteering","yoga","knitting"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":58,"level":"moderate"},"conscientiousness":{"score":54,"level":"moderate"},"extraversion":{"score":21,"level":"low"},"agreeableness":{"score":52,"level":"moderate"},"neuroticism":{"score":51,"level":"moderate"}}},"opinion":"<final end-user facing answer, 250-500 words>","reasoning_summary":"<full,step-by-step derivations>","tone":"<derived from persona traits>","citations":["<source name or org>"],"safety_notes":"<none>","used_persona_fields":["<list of persona fields referenced>"]}

mode = human
topic = How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
persona = {"age":38,"biological_sex":"female","gender":"woman","sexual_orientation":"heterosexual","occupation":"electrician","city_of_living":"Los Angeles","employment_status":"employed part-time","education_level":"upper secondary","parents_education":{"parent_1":"lower secondary","parent_2":"vocational diploma"},"marital_status":"married","children":3,"migration_status":"native American","psychological_feature":"depressed","religious_beliefs":"Atheist","time_onsocialmedia":"You have no social media","hobbies":["cycling","classical music","volunteering","yoga","knitting"],"ocean":{"openness":{"score":58,"level":"moderate"},"conscientiousness":{"score":54,"level":"moderate"},"extraversion":{"score":21,"level":"low"},"agreeableness":{"score":52,"level":"moderate"},"neuroticism":{"score":51,"level":"moderate"}}}

response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.",
  "opinion": "As a 38-year-old electrician living in Los Angeles, married with three kids, and dealing with depression, I see fake content on social media as a serious issue, even though I don't use it myself. My part-time work keeps me busy, but I hear about this from friends and family, and it worries me for my children's future. Managing fake content starts with education—teaching kids and adults to question what they see online, just like I double-check wiring diagrams at work to avoid mistakes. Schools and communities should offer workshops on media literacy, focusing on fact-checking and recognizing bias. Platforms need to step up too; they should use better algorithms to flag false information and be transparent about their efforts, maybe even facing penalties if they don't comply. As an atheist, I value evidence-based thinking, so I'd support laws that hold creators of harmful fake content accountable, similar to safety regulations in my field. Volunteering has shown me the power of community action, so I'd encourage people to report fake posts and support reliable sources. My low extraversion means I prefer quiet activities like knitting and cycling, but this issue pushes me to speak up because it affects mental health—my own depression makes me sensitive to how misinformation can spread anxiety. In short, it's about combining personal vigilance, tech solutions, and collective responsibility to build a safer online environment.",
  "reasoning_summary": "Derived opinion from persona traits: age 38 suggests maturity and concern for family; occupation as electrician implies practical, safety-focused thinking; depression and low extraversion indicate cautious, introspective approach; no social media use leads to outsider perspective emphasizing education and regulation; hobbies like volunteering and knitting support community-oriented solutions; atheism and moderate openness/conscientiousness favor evidence-based, structured responses; city of Los Angeles and native American status add context for diverse, urban challenges; marital status and children highlight protective instincts for online safety.",
  "tone": "Practical, concerned, introspective, community-focused, evidence-based",
  "citations": ["none"],
  "safety_notes": "none",
  "used_persona_fields": ["age", "occupation", "city_of_living", "employment_status", "marital_status", "children", "migration_status", "psychological_feature", "religious_beliefs", "time_onsocialmedia", "hobbies", "ocean"]
}

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As a 38-year-old electrician living in Los Angeles, married with three kids, and dealing with depression, I see fake content on social media as a serious issue, even though I don't use it myself. My part-time work keeps me busy, but I hear about this from friends and family, and it worries me for my children's future. Managing fake content starts with education—teaching kids and adults to question what they see online, just like I double-check wiring diagrams at work to avoid mistakes. Schools and communities should offer workshops on media literacy, focusing on fact-checking and recognizing bias. Platforms need to step up too; they should use better algorithms to flag false information and be transparent about their efforts, maybe even facing penalties if they don't comply. As an atheist, I value evidence-based thinking, so I'd support laws that hold creators of harmful fake content accountable, similar to safety regulations in my field. Volunteering has shown me the power of community action, so I'd encourage people to report fake posts and support reliable sources. My low extraversion means I prefer quiet activities like knitting and cycling, but this issue pushes me to speak up because it affects mental health—my own depression makes me sensitive to how misinformation can spread anxiety. In short, it's about combining personal vigilance, tech solutions, and collective responsibility to build a safer online environment.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Derived opinion from persona traits: age 38 suggests maturity and concern for family; occupation as electrician implies practical, safety-focused thinking; depression and low extraversion indicate cautious, introspective approach; no social media use leads to outsider perspective emphasizing education and regulation; hobbies like volunteering and knitting support community-oriented solutions; atheism and moderate openness/conscientiousness favor evidence-based, structured responses; city of Los Angeles and native American status add context for diverse, urban challenges; marital status and children highlight protective instincts for online safety.
